Lecture 6: Four Fundamental Subspaces ​

Topics: §2.3–2.4 — Transpose, Orthogonality Relationships, Four Fundamental Subspaces Date: Mar 23–26, 2026 Slides: PDF

🎯 Overview ​

In previous lectures, we learned:

  • Lecture 4: Subspaces have two languages (descriptive ↔ constructive)
  • Lecture 5: Solving equations bridges the two languages

Today, we discover deeper relationships between subspaces:

  1. When do combined operations AB preserve null spaces?
  2. What happens when we "flip" a production table (transpose)?
  3. How do the four fundamental subspaces of a matrix relate to each other?

1. Subspaces of Matrix Products ​

1.1 Setup: Combining Two Transformations ​

Consider two matrices that we compose:

Example

Example: Two Matrices B and A

B=(102011000)(3×3 matrix)A=(210100)(2×3 matrix)

Product AB:

AB=(210100)(102011000)=(215102)

Question: How do the column spaces and null spaces of A, B, and AB relate?

1.2 Theorem 1: Column Space of Product is Contained ​

Proposition

Theorem 1: Col(AB)⊆Col(A)

The column space of AB is always contained in the column space of A.

Proof:

Write B with columns b1,…,bn:

B=(|||b1b2⋯bn|||)

Then AB has columns:

AB=(|||Ab1Ab2⋯Abn|||)

Key observation: Each column of AB is Abj, which is a linear combination of the columns of A (with coefficients from bj).

Therefore, every column of AB lies in Col(A).

Since Col(AB) is spanned by the columns of AB, we have:

Col(AB)⊆Col(A)$$□:::remark∗∗Whythismakessense:Columnsof$AB$arelinearcombinationsofcolumnsof$A$∗∗RecallfromLecture1:the$j$−thcolumnof$AB$equals$A$timesthe$j$−thcolumnof$B$:$$(AB)j=Abj

But Abj is precisely a linear combination of A's columns, with coefficients from bj.

Example: If bj=(2−13) and A has columns a1,a2,a3, then:

Abj=2a1−1a2+3a3∈Col(A)

So every column of AB is built from A's columns! :::

Verification with our example:

Col(AB)=span{(21),(10),(52)}

Note that (52)=2(21)+1(10), so actually:

Col(AB)=span{(21),(10)}=R2

And indeed, Col(A)=span{(21),(10),(00)}=R2.

So Col(AB)⊆Col(A) ✓ (In this case, equality holds!)

1.3 Theorem 2: Null Space Inclusion ​

Proposition

Theorem 2: Null(B)⊆Null(AB)

The null space of B is always contained in the null space of AB.

Proof:

By definition:

  • Null(B)={x:Bx=0}
  • Null(AB)={x:(AB)x=0}

If x∈Null(B), then Bx=0. Thus:

(AB)x=A(Bx)=A(0)=0

Therefore x∈Null(AB), proving Null(B)⊆Null(AB). □

Remark

Why this makes sense: Linear dependence relations persist

Recall from Lecture 5: a vector x∈Null(B) records a linear dependence relation among the columns of B.

For example, if x=(2−10)∈Null(B), this says:

2b1−1b2+0b3=0

Now, what happens when we apply A to this relation?

A(2b1−1b2)=2(Ab1)−1(Ab2)=2(AB)1−1(AB)2

Since the original columns of B had a linear dependence, the transformed columns of AB inherit the same linear dependence!

General principle: Applying A preserves all linear relations that already existed in B's columns.

Could there be new linear dependencies in AB that weren't in B? That's the next theorem...

Verification with our example:

Null(B)={x:(102011000)x=0}

Solving (by inspection or cross-filling):

Null(B)=span{(−2−11)}

This records the linear dependence: −2b1−1b2+1b3=0 (where bj are columns of B).

Check: Does this linear dependence also hold for AB?

AB(−2−11)=(215102)(−2−11)=(00)$$✓So$Null(B)⊆Null(AB)$isconfirmed!∗∗Question∗∗:Is$Null(AB)$strictlylarger?Let′sfindout:$$Null(AB)={x:(215102)x=0}=span{(−2−11)}

In this example, Null(AB)=Null(B) (equality!). Why?

1.4 Theorem 3: When Are the Null Spaces Equal? ​

The key question: When is Null(AB)=Null(B) exactly (no extra "victims")?

Proposition

Theorem 3: The Equivalence

Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

This theorem has two directions. Let's prove both.

Direction (a): Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟹Null(AB)=Null(B)

Proof:

From Theorem 2, we already know Null(B)⊆Null(AB).

To show equality, we need Null(AB)⊆Null(B).

Take any x∈Null(AB). Then (AB)x=0, so A(Bx)=0.

This means Bx∈Null(A).

At the same time, Bx∈Col(B) (by definition of column space).

Hence, Bx∈Null(A)∩Col(B).

By assumption, Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}. Thus, Bx=0.

Therefore, x∈Null(B).

This proves Null(AB)⊆Null(B). Combined with Theorem 2, we have Null(AB)=Null(B). □

Direction (b): Null(AB)=Null(B)⟹Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}

Proof:

Assume Null(AB)=Null(B).

We want to show Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}.

Suppose v∈Null(A)∩Col(B).

Then v∈Null(A), so Av=0.

Also, v∈Col(B), so v=Bx for some x.

Combining these:

A(Bx)=0⇒(AB)x=0

Thus, x∈Null(AB). By hypothesis, Null(AB)=Null(B), so x∈Null(B).

Therefore, Bx=0, which implies v=0.

Hence, Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}. □

1.5 Understanding the Equivalence: Two Perspectives ​

The equivalence Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B) has a beautiful geometric interpretation.

Remark

Two Ways to Think About It

Perspective 1: Domain of B (where x lives)

Null(AB)=Null(B)

Meaning: All linear dependence relations among columns of AB were already present in columns of B. The transformation A didn't create any new dependencies.

Equivalently: If x satisfies (AB)x=0, then it already satisfied Bx=0.

Perspective 2: Codomain of B (where Bx lives)

Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}

Meaning: Among all vectors that B can produce (i.e., Col(B)), none of the nonzero ones land in Null(A).

Equivalently: If v=Bx for some x and Av=0, then v=0 (which forces Bx=0).

Why are these equivalent?

  • If Null(AB) is strictly larger than Null(B), there exists x with Bx≠0 but (AB)x=0
  • This means v=Bx is a nonzero vector in Col(B) that satisfies Av=0
  • So v∈Null(A)∩Col(B) with v≠0

Conversely:

  • If Null(A)∩Col(B) contains a nonzero vector v, write v=Bx for some x
  • Then (AB)x=A(Bx)=Av=0 but Bx=v≠0
  • So x∈Null(AB) but x∉Null(B)

Conclusion: The two conditions detect the same phenomenon from different viewpoints!

1.6 Summary of Matrix Product Subspaces ​

TIP

Three Key Relationships

  1. Col(AB)⊆Col(A)

    • Every column of AB is a linear combination of columns of A

  2. Null(B)⊆Null(AB)

    • Linear dependence relations among B's columns persist in AB's columns

  3. Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

    • No new dependencies created by A ⟺ No nonzero vector from Col(B) lands in Null(A)

2. Transpose: Flipping the Production Table ​

2.1 What is Transpose? ​

In our ingredient table framework, a matrix A represents:

  • Rows = raw materials (inputs)
  • Columns = products (outputs)
  • Entry Aij = how much material i is needed for product j

What if we flip our perspective? Instead of asking "how much material does each product need?", we ask "how much of each product uses a given material?"

This is the transpose.

Example

Example: Flipping the Ingredient Table

Original table A:

A=🥛☕🍵🍃002🍋001🫘024🐄101=(002001024101)

Interpretation: Rows = materials, Columns = products

  • Column 🥛: needs (0🍃, 0🍋, 0🫘, 1🐄)
  • Row 🍃: used in products with amounts (0, 0, 2)

Flipped table AT (transpose):

AT=🍃🍋🫘🐄🥛0001☕0020🍵2141=(000100202141)

New interpretation: Rows = products, Columns = materials

  • Row 🥛: contains amounts (0🍃, 0🍋, 0🫘, 1🐄) from each material
  • Column 🍃: distributed to products with amounts (0, 0, 2)

Notice: The rows of A become the columns of AT, and vice versa.

Formal definition:

Proposition

Definition: Transpose

For an m×n matrix A, its transpose AT is the n×m matrix obtained by:

(AT)ij=Aji

The rows of A become the columns of AT, and the columns of A become the rows of AT.

2.2 Properties of Transpose ​

Proposition

Three Key Properties of Transpose

  1. (AB)T=BTAT (order reverses)
  2. (A−1)T=(AT)−1 (transpose and inverse commute)
  3. (A+B)T=AT+BT (transpose is linear)

Proof of Property 1: (AB)T=BTAT

We verify entry-by-entry. The (i,j)-entry of (AB)T is:

(AB)ijT=(AB)ji=∑kAjkBki

The (i,j)-entry of BTAT is:

(BTAT)ij=∑k(BT)ik(AT)kj=∑kBkiAjk

Since multiplication of numbers is commutative, ∑kAjkBki=∑kBkiAjk. □

Remark

Why the order reverses (production table view)

In a two-stage production A⋅B:

  • B: raw materials → semi-products
  • A: semi-products → final products

From the raw materials' perspective (BT): Each material distributes to various semi-products.

From the final products' perspective (AT): Each product sources from various semi-products.

To trace backwards from final products to raw materials, we must:

  1. First follow AT: final product → semi-products it uses
  2. Then follow BT: semi-products → raw materials they need

Hence (AB)T=BTAT — the order flips!

Proof of Property 2: (A−1)T=(AT)−1 (assuming A is invertible)

We show that AT⋅(A−1)T=I.

Starting from A⋅A−1=I, transpose both sides:

(A⋅A−1)T=IT=I

Using Property 1:

(A−1)T⋅AT=I

Similarly, (A−1⋅A)T=I gives AT⋅(A−1)T=I.

Therefore, (A−1)T is the inverse of AT, so (A−1)T=(AT)−1. □

Proof of Property 3: (A+B)T=AT+BT

Entry-by-entry: \begin{align*} (A + B)^T_{ij} &= (A + B){ji} \ &= A + B_{ji} \ &= (A^T){ij} + (B^T) \ &= (A^T + B^T)_{ij} \end

Therefore (A+B)T=AT+BT. □

2.3 Symmetric Matrices ​

Proposition

Definition: Symmetric Matrix

A matrix S is symmetric if ST=S.

Meaning: The ingredient table looks the same when flipped!

Example

Example: A Symmetric Matrix

S=(123245356)

Flipping rows ↔ columns gives the same matrix:

ST=(123245356)=S

Proposition

Theorem: AAT and ATA are Always Symmetric

For any matrix A:

  1. AAT is symmetric
  2. ATA is symmetric

Proof:

Using property (AB)T=BTAT:

(AAT)T=(AT)TAT=AAT

So AAT is symmetric.

Similarly:

(ATA)T=AT(AT)T=ATA

So ATA is symmetric. □

Example

Verification with a Concrete Matrix

A=(123456),AT=(135246)

Compute AAT:

AAT=(123456)(135246)=(51117112539173961)

Check symmetry: (AAT)T=AAT ✓ (matrix is symmetric about diagonal)

Compute ATA:

ATA=(135246)(123456)=(35444456)

Check symmetry: (ATA)T=ATA ✓

Remark

Change of Perspective

So far, we have used the ingredient table metaphor to understand transpose: flipping the table swaps the roles of materials and products.

From this point forward, we work with abstract matrices A, B, C without referring back to coffee shops. The key properties we've established ((AB)T=BTAT, symmetry of AAT, etc.) apply to all matrices, and we can now use them to discover deeper relationships.

The next section reveals a beautiful geometric relationship: orthogonality between certain subspaces.

3. Orthogonality Relationships of the Four Fundamental Subspaces ​

3.1 The Four Fundamental Subspaces ​

For any m×n matrix A, there are four fundamental subspaces:

SubspaceDefinitionLives inDimension
Col(A){Ax:x∈Rn}Rm (codomain)rank(A)
Null(A){x:Ax=0}Rn (domain)n−rank(A)
Col(AT){ATy:y∈Rm}Rn (domain)rank(AT)=rank(A)
Null(AT){y:ATy=0}Rm (codomain)m−rank(A)

Key observations:

  1. Two subspaces in Rn (domain):

    • Null(A) (descriptive: solutions to Ax=0)
    • Col(AT) (constructive: linear combinations of rows of A)

  2. Two subspaces in Rm (codomain):

    • Col(A) (constructive: linear combinations of columns of A)
    • Null(AT) (descriptive: solutions to ATy=0)

Today's main discovery: These pairs are orthogonal to each other!

3.2 Theorem: Col(AT)⊥Null(A) ​

Proposition

Theorem 4: Col(AT) is Orthogonal to Null(A)

Every vector in Col(AT) is orthogonal to every vector in Null(A).

Precisely: For any x∈Null(A) and any y∈Col(AT),

yTx=0

Proof (Inner Product View):

Let x∈Null(A) and y∈Col(AT).

By definition:

  • x∈Null(A) means Ax=0
  • y∈Col(AT) means y=ATz for some z∈Rm

Compute the inner product:

yTx=(ATz)Tx=zT(AT)Tx=zTAx

Since x∈Null(A), we have Ax=0. Therefore:

zTAx=zT0=0

Thus, yTx=0, proving orthogonality. □

Proof (Geometric View: Rows as Normal Vectors):

The equation Ax=0 can be written row-by-row:

(—r1T——r2T—⋮—rmT—)x=(r1Txr2Tx⋮rmTx)=0

This means:

r1Tx=0,r2Tx=0,…,rmTx=0

Interpretation: Each row ri of A is perpendicular to every vector x∈Null(A).

But the rows of A are exactly the columns of AT!

Therefore, Col(AT)=span{r1,r2,…,rm} consists of vectors perpendicular to Null(A). □

Example

Concrete Example

A=(123246)

Find Null(A):

Ax=0⟹{x1+2x2+3x3=02x1+4x2+6x3=0

The second equation is 2× the first, so we have one independent equation:

x1+2x2+3x3=0

Basis for Null(A):

Null(A)=span{(−210),(−301)}

Find Col(AT):

AT=(122436)Col(AT)=span{(123),(246)}=span{(123)}

(The second vector is 2× the first.)

Check orthogonality:

(123)T(−210)=−2+2+0=0✓(123)T(−301)=−3+0+3=0✓

Indeed, Col(AT)⊥Null(A)!

3.3 Theorem: Col(A)⊥Null(AT) ​

Proposition

Theorem 5: Col(A) is Orthogonal to Null(AT)

Every vector in Col(A) is orthogonal to every vector in Null(AT).

Precisely: For any x∈Col(A) and any y∈Null(AT),

yTx=0

Proof:

Let y∈Null(AT) and x∈Col(A).

By definition:

  • y∈Null(AT) means ATy=0
  • x∈Col(A) means x=Az for some z∈Rn

Compute the inner product:

yTx=yT(Az)=(ATy)Tz

Since y∈Null(AT), we have ATy=0. Therefore:

(ATy)Tz=0Tz=0

Thus, yTx=0, proving orthogonality. □

Remark

Symmetry of the Orthogonality Relationships

Notice the beautiful duality:

  • Theorem 4: Col(AT)⊥Null(A) (both live in Rn)
  • Theorem 5: Col(A)⊥Null(AT) (both live in Rm)

These are not two separate facts — they are the same theorem applied to A and AT!

If you apply Theorem 4 to the matrix AT (instead of A), you get:

Col((AT)T)⊥Null(AT)⟹Col(A)⊥Null(AT)

which is exactly Theorem 5!

This reveals a deep symmetry in the structure of linear algebra.

Example

Concrete Example (Dual of Previous)

Using the same matrix:

A=(123246)

Find Col(A):

Col(A)=span{(12),(24),(36)}=span{(12)}

Find Null(AT):

ATy=0⟹(122436)(y1y2)=0⟹{y1+2y2=02y1+4y2=03y1+6y2=0

All three equations are proportional: y1+2y2=0.

Basis:

Null(AT)=span{(−21)}

Check orthogonality:

(−21)T(12)=−2+2=0✓

Indeed, Col(A)⊥Null(AT)!

3.4 The Big Picture: Four Subspaces ​

TIP

The Four Fundamental Subspaces and Their Relationships

For an m×n matrix A with rank(A)=r:

In Rn (domain):

  • Col(AT) (dimension r) ⊥ Null(A) (dimension n−r)
  • Together they "fill" Rn: dim⁡(Col(AT))+dim⁡(Null(A))=r+(n−r)=n

In Rm (codomain):

  • Col(A) (dimension r) ⊥ Null(AT) (dimension m−r)
  • Together they "fill" Rm: dim⁡(Col(A))+dim⁡(Null(AT))=r+(m−r)=m

Orthogonality relationships:

Col(AT)⊥Null(A)(both in Rn)Col(A)⊥Null(AT)(both in Rm)

These relationships are foundational for understanding projections, least squares, and the geometry of linear transformations.

4. Dimension Relationships ​

4.1 Row Rank Equals Column Rank ​

Proposition

Theorem 6: rank(A)=rank(AT)

The dimension of Col(A) equals the dimension of Col(AT).

In other words: Column rank = Row rank.

Proof idea (using cross-filling):

Recall from Lecture 3 that any matrix can be decomposed via cross-filling:

A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr=UV

where:

  • U is m×r with columns {u1,…,ur} (the cross-columns)
  • V is r×n with rows {v1T,…,vrT} (the cross-rows)
  • r=rank(A) = number of rank-1 pieces

Column rank: Col(A)=Col(UV)=Col(U), so dim⁡(Col(A))=r.

Row rank: The rows of A are linear combinations of the rows of V (since A=UV). The rows of V span the same space as the rows of A. Since V has r linearly independent rows, dim⁡(Col(AT))=r.

Therefore, rank(A)=rank(AT)=r. □

(A complete rigorous proof requires showing the rows of V are linearly independent, which follows from the cross-filling construction. We will not go into full detail here, but the key idea is that cross-filling produces exactly r independent rows and r independent columns.)

4.2 Dimension Formulas ​

From the rank-nullity theorem (Lecture 5) and the orthogonality relationships, we have:

Proposition

Dimension Relationships for the Four Fundamental Subspaces

For an m×n matrix A with rank(A)=r:

In Rn (domain):

dim⁡(Col(AT))+dim⁡(Null(A))=r+(n−r)=n

In Rm (codomain):

dim⁡(Col(A))+dim⁡(Null(AT))=r+(m−r)=m

These formulas confirm: The orthogonal pairs (Col(AT),Null(A)) and (Col(A),Null(AT)) together span their entire ambient spaces.

5. Summary ​

TIP

What We Learned

1. Subspaces of Matrix Products (§1):

  • Col(AB)⊆Col(A)
  • Null(B)⊆Null(AB)
  • Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

2. Transpose (§2):

  • (AB)T=BTAT (order reverses)
  • (A−1)T=(AT)−1
  • AAT and ATA are always symmetric

3. Four Fundamental Subspaces (§3):

  • Col(AT)⊥Null(A) (in Rn)
  • Col(A)⊥Null(AT) (in Rm)

4. Dimension Relationships (§4):

  • rank(A)=rank(AT)
  • dim⁡(Col(AT))+dim⁡(Null(A))=n
  • dim⁡(Col(A))+dim⁡(Null(AT))=m

Key Insight: The four fundamental subspaces form two orthogonal pairs that completely describe the geometry of a linear transformation.

Further Reading ​

  • The orthogonality relationships will be crucial for understanding projections (Lecture 7)
  • The "two cities" framework connects to the fundamental theorem of linear algebra
  • Transpose will reappear in symmetric matrices and spectral decomposition (later lectures)
